description: The provided image is irrelevant to the research content of the paper focusing on Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel. It shows a software interface button with the text 'Check for updates' below a graphic icon: a red bookmark-shaped pattern is placed inside an incomplete circular ring, which is colored blue in the upper half and yellow in the lower half. No material science related information including microstructure characterization results, mechanical property data, processing routes, phase information, or deformation mechanism analysis content can be obtained from this image, so it cannot support any scientific conclusions of the studied cryogenic steel system.

This figure characterizes the initial microstructure of Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel after solution treatment at 1050 °C for 2 h followed by oil quenching. Subfigure (a) is the EBSD phase map, showing FCC γ austenite as the matrix (average grain size 10.8 μm, 97 vol.%) with ~3 vol.% BCC α phase (average grain size 4.7 μm) distributed along γ grain boundaries, with white lines marking annealing twin boundaries. Subfigure (b) is the EBSD KAM map, indicating an average geometrically necessary dislocation density of 1.6×10¹³ m⁻². Subfigure (c) is the EBSD IPF map along the rolling direction, revealing a very weak initial texture. Subfigures (d), (e), (f) are selected area electron diffraction patterns along [100]γ, [110]γ, and [110]MC zone axes, respectively, confirming the presence of L1₂-type short-range ordering, lattice modulation from solute concentration fluctuations, and MC carbide precipitates. These results provide baseline microstructural information for analyzing the steel’s unusual deformation twinning mechanism in its high stacking fault energy state.

This figure displays the mechanical properties of Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel tested at room temperature (RT, black curves) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT, red curves). Subplot (a) shows tensile engineering stress-strain curves: LNT achieves higher yield strength (497 MPa vs 251 MPa at RT), ultimate tensile strength (945 MPa vs 580 MPa at RT), and elongation (75.5% vs 52.0% at RT). Subplot (b) presents true stress-strain curves and corresponding strain hardening rate curves: the strain hardening process at RT has 2 stages while LNT exhibits 4 stages, with LNT showing significantly higher strain hardening rate across most strain ranges. Subplot (c) provides instrumented Charpy impact force-displacement and energy-displacement curves: crack initiation energy Wm is 79.28 J at RT and 99.19 J at LNT (similar level), while crack propagation energy Wp decreases from 251.33 J at RT to 156.71 J at LNT, indicating total impact energy is dominated by crack propagation energy. This figure demonstrates the excellent cryogenic mechanical performance of the studied steel, with the different hardening and impact behaviors corresponding to distinct deformation mechanisms (earlier deformation twinning at LNT contributes to higher simultaneous strength and ductility).

This figure presents the evolution of Schmid factor as a function of engineering tensile strain for Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel, tested at room temperature (RT) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT). Four curves are included: grey square for RT {111}<110> slip system of γ austenite, red circle for RT {111}<112> slip system of γ austenite, blue triangle for LNT {111}<110> slip system of γ austenite, green inverted triangle for LNT {111}<112> slip system of γ austenite. All Schmid factors first increase to a peak at ~0.1 engineering strain, with the highest peak value of ~0.45 for the LNT {111}<112> system, then generally decrease with further increasing strain. Schmid factors of the same slip system at LNT are overall higher than those at RT, indicating higher activation tendency of slip and deformation twinning at cryogenic temperature, which supports the simultaneously enhanced strength and ductility of the studied steel at LNT.

This figure displays EBSD-measured inverse pole figures (IPFs) of austenite along the tensile axis (TA) for Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel after different tensile strains. Subplot (a) shows IPFs at room temperature (RT) under engineering strains of 0%, 3%, 28% and 44%, with maximum texture intensity of 2.55, 2.74, 3.16 and 4.81 respectively. Subplot (b) shows IPFs at liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT, -196 °C) under engineering strains of 3%, 10%, 28% and 55%, with maximum texture intensity of 2.01, 2.42, 2.57 and 6.68 respectively. Initial texture is weak, and texture intensity increases gradually with rising tensile strain, finally forming a strong <111>//TA texture and weak <100>//TA texture at both temperatures. At equivalent strain, <111>//TA texture intensity is slightly higher at RT than LNT, while at maximum uniform elongation, the texture intensity is significantly higher at LNT due to its larger attainable tensile strain. These results verify the texture evolution law during tensile deformation, correlating with the excellent cryogenic mechanical properties and deformation twinning mechanism of the studied steel.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization results of Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel after tensile deformation at room temperature (RT) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT). The top row (a1-d1) are inverse pole figures along the tensile axis (TA), classifying grains into Type I (red dots, <001> orientation), Type II (green dots, <101> orientation) and Type III (blue dots, <111> orientation), with regions where twinning Schmid factor mtw < slip Schmid factor ms labeled. a1 corresponds to RT 38% engineering strain, b1, c1, d1 correspond to LNT 28%, 38%, 55% engineering strain respectively. The bottom row (a2-d2) are EBSD inverse pole figure orientation maps of corresponding samples, with a scale bar of 25 μm. Key observations include: deformation twinning fraction at LNT increases with rising strain; twinning preferentially forms in Type II and Type III grains, with Type II grains tending to form single-orientation twins while Type III grains form multi-orientation twins, and few twins in Type I grains. These results demonstrate that deformation twinning with strong grain orientation dependence is a critical deformation mechanism contributing to the excellent cryogenic mechanical properties of the studied steel.

This set of figures compares the dislocation glide induced grain subdivision behavior of Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel deformed to 28% engineering strain at room temperature (RT, subfigures a-c) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT, subfigures d-f). Subfigures (a, d) are inverse pole figure (IPF) maps along the tensile axis (TA) with a 25 μm scale bar, showing grain orientation distribution. Subfigures (b, e) are Kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps with a 25 μm scale bar, color-coded for local misorientation from 0° to 5°, reflecting local dislocation density distribution. Subfigures (c, f) are TA-aligned inverse pole figures mapping dislocation glide traces, where green dots represent single glide and blue dots represent double glide, with regions divided by twinning tendency criteria (comparison of m_tw and m_s). The LNT deformed sample exhibits higher local misorientation and higher double glide fraction than the RT sample, indicating more severe dislocation pile-up and more active multiple slip at cryogenic temperature, supporting that coexistence of dislocation hardening and deformation twinning at LNT improves strength and ductility simultaneously.

This set of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, supplemented with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns as insets, show deformation-induced microstructures of Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel after room temperature tensile deformation at different strains: (a) 3% strain with dislocation multi-junctions, nodes and pairs; (b,c) 10% strain with Lomer-Cottrell locks and high-density dislocation walls; (d-f) 20% strain with Taylor lattices, cell blocks and dislocation cells formed along {111} slip planes, where grains oriented close to <100>//tensile axis preferentially form dislocation cells while other orientations form cell blocks; (g-j) 28% strain with refined cell blocks/dislocation cells, first observed deformation twins along cell block walls, plus microbands; (k,l) 38% strain with further developed but low-fraction deformation twins. All subgraphs have a 500 nm scale bar. The figure demonstrates that twinning in this high stacking fault energy steel is orientation-dependent, induced by stress concentration from planar dislocation substructures promoted by short-range ordering.

This figure presents TEM characterization of deformation-induced microstructures in Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel under different tensile strains at liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT). Subfigures correspond to strains of 3% (a,b), 10% (c-e), 20% (f-i), 28% (j-o), 38% (p,q), and 55% (r,t). At 3% strain, planar slip features including dislocation pairs, dislocation nodes, and stacking faults at annealed twin boundaries are observed. Deformation twins nucleate at 10% strain with an average twin bundle width of ~5 nm. At 20% strain, dislocation cell blocks and dislocation cells form, with a low fraction of deformation twins. At 28% strain, dislocation substructures are refined, wide microbands (~390 nm width) and fine microbands (~137 nm width) appear, while twin fraction remains low. A large number of deformation twins are activated at 38% strain, and secondary twins with more stacking faults are observed at 55% strain. The figure confirms that deformation twinning initiates at as low as 10% tensile strain at LNT, reveals the evolution of deformation mechanisms from dislocation slip to twinning dominated plasticity with increasing strain, and supports the underlying mechanism for the excellent cryogenic mechanical properties of the studied steel.

This figure presents the deformation microstructure and fracture features of Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density austenitic duplex steel after Charpy impact tests at room temperature (RT, a-e) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT, f-j). (a,f) are macroscopic fracture photos: the RT sample has more subordinate cracks and more obvious necking than the LNT sample, corresponding to higher crack propagation energy at RT. (b,g) are EBSD phase maps near the fracture, confirming no phase transformation of the γ austenite matrix (with ~3 vol.% α ferrite), and microcracks initiate at γ/α interfaces in the LNT sample. (c,h) are EBSD inverse pole figure maps along the impact direction, showing LNT promotes deformation twinning. (d,i) are TEM images ~1000μm from the fracture, exhibiting twins, dislocation cells and microbands, with SAED patterns verifying the twin structure. (e,j) are SEM fracture morphologies, both showing ductile fracture with micro/submicron dimples. This figure verifies the steel retains excellent ductile fracture resistance at both temperatures, with only ~23% impact energy reduction at LNT, confirming its promising cryogenic application potential.

This figure contains two scatter plots comparing the cryogenic mechanical properties of the newly developed Fe35Mn6Al0.1C low-carbon low-density steel (marked as present work) with other reported cryogenic alloys at liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT). Subplot (a) plots Charpy impact energy (J, LNT) against specific yield strength (MPa·cm³·g⁻¹), while subplot (b) plots total elongation (%, LNT) against specific yield strength. The compared alloys include high carbon lightweight steels, TWIP steels, low-carbon high manganese steels, 304 stainless steel, 9Ni steel, and Al0.1CoCrFeNi high-entropy alloy. The results show that the developed steel has higher Charpy impact energy than high carbon lightweight steels and TWIP steels, superior specific yield strength than low-carbon high manganese steels and 304 stainless steel, and total elongation comparable to austenite TWIP steels, nearly 3 times higher than 9Ni steel. This figure validates that the developed low-cost low-carbon low-density steel has excellent comprehensive cryogenic mechanical properties, showing great application potential for cryogenic scenarios.

This figure investigates the austenite phase stability of Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel. Subplot (a) presents XRD profiles of specimens tensile deformed at liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT) with engineering strains of 0%, 3%, 10%, 28%, and 55%, respectively. Characteristic diffraction peaks correspond to FCC γ-austenite ((111)γ, (200)γ, (220)γ) and minor BCC α-ferrite ((110)α), with austenite volume fractions remaining stable at ~95.6-97.3% regardless of deformation strain. Subplot (b) shows the equilibrium phase diagram calculated via Thermal-Calc software with TCFE12 database, plotting molar fractions of austenite and ferrite against temperature from 950℃ to 1200℃, confirming the small stable ferrite fraction at room/low temperature consistent with XRD results. The figure collectively demonstrates that no deformation-induced phase transformation occurs during LNT deformation, supporting that deformation twinning is the dominant deformation mechanism for this steel.

This figure is a schematic diagram of Taylor factor M distribution along the tensile axis (TA) direction in the designed Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel. The three vertices of the triangular diagram correspond to three crystallographic orientations: <001> orientation with M value of 2.30, <111> orientation with M value of 3.64, and <101> orientation with M value of 3.64. The diagram is divided into different regions: grains with M < 2.7, intermediate M region, and grains with M > 3.06, where regions with intermediate and high M are marked as "Twins", indicating that deformation twinning is preferentially activated in grains with higher Taylor factor. This schematic supports the conclusion that stacking fault energy is not the only decisive factor controlling deformation twinning in this steel, and twinning exhibits strong orientation dependence.
